briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for July 10, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Intensifying Global Climate Crisis Warnings
sections.content: According to news reported today, record-breaking heatwaves and extreme weather phenomena are intensifying globally. Western European countries, including France and the UK, are suffering from heatwaves, recording their highest-ever temperatures, which clearly demonstrates a lack of preparedness for climate change [1, 5, 18, 74, 101, 103]. Furthermore, experts continue to warn that rising temperatures are increasing torrential rainfall and 'water bombs,' and that climate change is causing more extreme weather than El Niño [8, 66, 92, 95]. These abnormal weather patterns are blurring regional and seasonal boundaries [10], and research findings have also been released indicating that the climate crisis is impacting human health and posing a threat to marine life [7, 69, 85]. Some scientists are even raising the possibility that the timeline for global warming may have been miscalculated, emphasizing the urgency of the crisis [71].

sections.title: 🇰🇷🌱 Accelerating Domestic and International Carbon Neutrality and Energy Transition
sections.content: Amidst this climate crisis, domestic and international efforts for carbon neutrality and energy transition continue to expand. The Republic of Korea and Mongolia have signed an MOU for cooperation in climate change and forestry, accelerating energy transition by supporting renewable energy development and power grid expansion [2, 16, 19, 23, 48, 50, 51, 56, 60, 63]. Domestically, Incheon Metropolitan City plans to accumulate funds for climate crisis response starting next year and is concretizing its carbon neutrality strategy for coastal cities, aiming to become a leading carbon-neutral city by 2045 through the expansion of offshore wind power and blue carbon [21, 29, 31, 35, 37, 43, 44, 45, 46, 47]. Support projects for the AI and carbon-neutral transition of industrial complexes nationwide are gaining momentum [24, 27, 30], and various technological and policy efforts are ongoing, such as the growing recognition of 'sea forests' as a new solution for carbon neutrality [34, 38].

sections.title: ⚠️ Climate Change: Impacts on Ecosystems and Social Systems
sections.content: Climate change is having widespread and severe impacts across our ecosystems and social systems. News has emerged that 4.6 billion tons of grain have been lost globally due to the climate crisis, exacerbating concerns about food security [15]. Furthermore, research has been published indicating that household water bills in some cities could double if climate change continues, warning of potential water shortages and increased economic burdens [67, 68]. In terms of ecosystems, the UK's curlew population has decreased by 89%, putting it at risk of extinction [82], and Mauritania's fishing heritage is also threatened by climate change and overfishing [93]. The winter sports industry is also affected, with ski resorts in France's Vosges region deciding to close ski lifts due to climate change [100], illustrating changes and crises observed across various industries.

sections.title: 🌱 Climate Action and Adaptation Efforts with Future Generations
sections.content: Amidst these challenges, various domestic and international efforts are underway to respond to and adapt to the climate crisis alongside future generations. Domestically, the Wonju Climate Change Promotion Center is offering eco-friendly moss ball making and carbon neutrality experiential education. Chungju City and Pohang City are also hosting climate change academies and climate labs for youth, aiming to raise climate awareness among future generations and explore solutions [4, 6, 9, 11, 12, 13, 42]. Citizen-led carbon neutrality initiatives through the establishment of social cooperatives are also active [26, 36]. Meanwhile, industrial adaptation examples are emerging, such as the shift in Busan where high-grade bluefin tuna, previously exported to Japan due to climate change, is now being distributed to domestic tables [3, 14, 17]. Internationally, UN Climate Change has evaluated Jamaica's climate resilience plan as a model for other nations [86], and Indonesian women seaweed farmers are demonstrating efforts to adapt to climate change, conveying a message of hope [96].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights both the severity of the climate crisis and the coexistence of various domestic and international efforts to overcome it. This is a time that demands sustained attention and action. This concludes today's climate briefing.
